coredata.dc:title: Novel lignin-derived carbon dots nanozyme cascade-amplified for ratiometric fluorescence detection of xanthine oxidase and intracellular imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Yin, Chenhui
Sun, Qijun
Wu, Meng
Yu, Xueling
Niu, Na
Chen, Ligang
coredata.dc:description: The development of lignin-derived carbon dots with dual functionality of fluorescence and enzyme-like activity was realized by hydrochloric acid mediated carbon dots modification, and then a ratiometric fluorescence sensing strategy to detect xanthine oxidase activity was constructed. Sodium lignosulfonate (LS) was used as the carbon source and modified lignin carbon dots (CDs) with both fluorescence and peroxidase-like activities were prepared. DFT theoretical calculations and experiments demonstrated that carbonyl, carboxyl, and chloride on the surface of carbon dots were potential active sites to catalyze production of ·OH from H2O2. Xanthine oxidase generated H2O2 in the process of catalyzing the production of uric acid from the substrate xanthine. The ·OH produced via LS-CDs catalyzed H2O2 oxidized residual o-phenylenediamine (OPD) groups on surface of OPD-CDs to enhance yellow fluorescence, accompanied by blue fluorescence quenching of LS-CDs. The realization of a highly sensitive, highly selective, and visualized ratiometric fluorescence sensing strategy was achieved. The linear range of the established fluorescence sensing system for xanthine oxidase was 0.5–120 U/L with detection limit of 0.13 U/L. The cascade reaction signal transduction improves the designability of sensing method. Finally, the system successfully realized the sensing of xanthine oxidase in serum and cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Xanthine oxidase
Lignin
Carbon dots
Enzymatic cascade
Ratiometric fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Hydrogen peroxide and xanthine oxidase detection
Ratiometric fluorescence detection xanthine oxidase in real samples
Studies on the catalytic activity of LS-CDs
Studies on the catalytic site of LS-CDs
Feasibility study and xanthine oxidase assay sensing
Real sample detection and method comparison
Imaging studies of intracellular xanthine oxidase

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project was funded by Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (LH2022B002), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2572022DJ01), 111 Project (B20088), Heilongjiang Touyan Innovation Team Program (Tree Genetics and Breeding Innovation Team). We would like to acknowledge the technical support from Analysis and Testing Center of Northeast Forestry University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: . (A) TEM images and (B) particle size distribution of LS-CDs. (C) FT-IR and (D) XPS spectra of LS-CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of LS-CDs: (E) C 1 s, (F) O 1 s and (G) Cl 2p. (H) UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation and fluorescence emission spectra of LS-CDs (inset: photo of LS-CDs). (H) Emission spectra of LS-CDs under different excitation wavelengths.

. (A) An illustration of TMB oxidation by LS-CDs and peroxidase. (B) UV spectra and photographs of chromogenic substrates and corresponding oxides in systems containing LS-CDs and H2O2. (C) UV spectra and photographs of different reaction systems. Factors affecting peroxidase-like activity of LS-CDs: (D) pH, (E) temperature. (F) Michaelis-Menten and (G) Lineweaver-Burk curve by changing the concentrations of H2O2. (H) Michaelis-Menten and (I) Lineweaver-Burk curve by changing the concentrations of TMB.

(A) Local surface planar structure of optimized LS-CDs. (B) Stereoscopic structure of LS-CDs with atomic numbers. (C) Charge distribution in Mulliken. (D) HOMO and LUMO orbitals. (E) molecular electrostatic potential image. (F) Effects of different free radical scavenger. (G) ESR spectra of catalytic system. (H) Changes of LS-CDs peroxidase activity after selective inactivation of surface oxygen-containing functional groups.

(A) Feasibility of ratiometric fluorescence analysis for XOD and corresponding color changes. (B) Fluorescence spectra with H2O2 concentration. (C) The relationship between F565/F454 and H2O2 concentration. (D) Fluorescence spectra with XOD concentration. (E) The relationship between F565/F454 and XOD concentration. (F) The corresponding fluorescence photos. (G) CIE chromaticity diagram. (H) Fluorescence response to a variety of coexisting substances. (I) Fluorescence response after the addition of multiple coexisting substances when in presence of XOD.

(A) Fluorescence microscopy images of HeLa cells with (a) LS-CDs/OPD-CDs, (b) LS-CDs/OPD-CDs+PMA, (c) LS-CDs/OPD-CDs+PMA+NAC, (d) LS-CDs/OPD-CDs/xanthin/XOD, (e) LS-CDs/OPD-CDs/xanthin/XOD+NAC and HeLa cells. Scale: 50 µm. (B) Blue and (C) Green fluorescence channel signal intensity curves. (D) Corresponding fluorescence intensity ratios.
Synthesis, catalytic sites of LS-CDs and application.

head.ce:title: Novel lignin-derived carbon dots nanozyme cascade-amplified for ratiometric fluorescence detection of xanthine oxidase and intracellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology, Northeast Forestry University, 26 Hexing Road, Harbin 150040, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology, Northeast Forestry University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology, Northeast Forestry University, 26 Hexing Road, Harbin, 150040, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of lignin-derived carbon dots with dual functionality of fluorescence and enzyme-like activity was realized by hydrochloric acid mediated carbon dots modification, and then a ratiometric fluorescence sensing strategy to detect xanthine oxidase activity was constructed. Sodium lignosulfonate (LS) was used as the carbon source and modified lignin carbon dots (CDs) with both fluorescence and peroxidase-like activities were prepared. DFT theoretical calculations and experiments demonstrated that carbonyl, carboxyl, and chloride on the surface of carbon dots were potential active sites to catalyze production of ·OH from H2O2. Xanthine oxidase generated H2O2 in the process of catalyzing the production of uric acid from the substrate xanthine. The ·OH produced via LS-CDs catalyzed H2O2 oxidized residual o-phenylenediamine (OPD) groups on surface of OPD-CDs to enhance yellow fluorescence, accompanied by blue fluorescence quenching of LS-CDs. The realization of a highly sensitive, highly selective, and visualized ratiometric fluorescence sensing strategy was achieved. The linear range of the established fluorescence sensing system for xanthine oxidase was 0.5–120 U/L with detection limit of 0.13 U/L. The cascade reaction signal transduction improves the designability of sensing method. Finally, the system successfully realized the sensing of xanthine oxidase in serum and cells.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Xanthine oxidase plays an important role in purine metabolism[1]. There is growing evidence that xanthine oxidase (XOD) activity is associated with diseases such as liver injury, heart failure and gout[2,3]. Therefore, it is crucial to closely monitor XOD levels in diagnosis and treatment of diseases. So far, several methods have been attempted for detecting XOD, including electrochemical methods[4], high-performance liquid chromatography[5], and colorimetric methods[6]. However, these methods are difficult to detect in real time without harming biological samples. Fluorescence methods are favored for their high sensitivity, ease of operation, time saving, and real-time detection of cells, showing bright prospects in biological analysis[7]. However, most of them rely on single fluorescent signal and inevitably face interference from environmental/background factors, resulting in false positive results. By recording the ratio of fluorescence intensity of two different emission signals, the ratiometric fluorescence strategy can reduce these problems to a large extent, and achieve more accurate and efficient detection, which is more suitable for practical applications. However, in some strategies the target is not sensitive enough for response units and novel detection methods need to be developed.

Enzyme cascade reactions consist of at least two consecutive processes. Since the reaction is spontaneous and no product separation occurs, it is an efficient method to amplify detection signal by introducing an enzyme cascade into detection system[8]. Nanozymes have shown good stability, high catalytic activity, and are promising natural enzyme substitutes[9]. Their outstanding physical and chemical properties (e.g., high surface energy, excellent optical and photothermal conversion properties), as well as easy preparation and storage, also extend their applications[10,37]. As a result, a great deal of research has been conducted. In view of the above advantages, its application gradually from environmental protection to chemical, food, agriculture, bio-pharmaceutical and pharmaceutical fields. After more than ten years' investigation, many kinds of nanozymes have been developed, such as metal-like, carbon-based and metal-organic framework materials[11,36]. Many nanostructured materials have been used as mediators in various reaction systems, including colorimetric, electrochemical and chemiluminescent biosensors[12]. However, the application of nanozymes in fluorescence sensing, especially in ratiometric fluorescence, has not received sufficient attention up to now. For example, Li et al. developed GeO2 nanozymes with peroxidase and designed an organophosphate pesticide detection strategy based on the characteristics of thiocholine degradation of GeO2 nanozymes with corresponding enzymatic reactions[13]. However, these materials have poor biocompatibility, high preparation cost and complicated preparation process. Therefore, there is an urgent need to prepare peroxide-like nanozymes with simple synthesis, low cost and good biocompatibility. Recently, more and more attention has been paid to preparation of carbon based nanozymes[14]. Carbon dots (CDs), as zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, have remarkable and excellent fluorescence properties[15,38]. Acidification during carbon dots process can introduce new defect energy levels and surface morphology to flexibly regulate fluorescence properties[16]. People have realized sensing of various metal ions using CDs. However, there are fewer domestic and international research reports on acidification to improve intrinsic catalytic properties of CDs.

Lignin is a kind of natural macromolecule with phenylpropane as its basic structural unit, which is the second largest biomass resource in plants[17]. There are rich and special phenolic hydroxyl groups in the structure, which can increase the utilization of lignin[18]. Because of its intrinsic properties, lignin can be transformed into high value nanoscale fluorescent materials in a number of ways. Our groups[19,20] prepared lignin-derived CDs for bioimaging and sensing based on their fluorescent properties. However, the synthesis, fluorescence and catalytic mechanism of lignin derivatives are less studied. The study of lignin and CDs is essential for synthesis and function of lignin-CDs. In addition, hydrothermal preparation of CDs has advantages, such as low cost, environmentally friendly, easy to operate, and even particle size, which can be used to synthesize CDs. Considering that lignin is difficult to degrade, hydrochloric acid (HCl) accelerated hydrolytic polymerization of lignin[21]. However, the functionalization of lignin-derived CDs using hydrochloric acid to prepare lignin CDs with catalytic function has never been reported.

In this study, lignin CDs nanozymes (LS-CDs) with fluorescence and catalytic activity were innovatively fabricated. A peroxidase-like cascade reaction system was constructed for proportional fluorescence detection of biomolecules. The fluorescence and peroxidase-like properties of LS-CDs were firstly investigated. Possible active centers and catalytic mechanism were analyzed by density functional theory combined with experiments. OPD-CDs were introduced to construct a cascade reaction system for proportional fluorescence sensing. When xanthine is catalyzed by XOD, the catalytic reaction produces H2O2. The released H2O2 was catalyzed by LS-CDs to ·OH, which further oxidized OPD-derived CDs and enhanced yellow fluorescence. At the same time, blue fluorescence of LS-CDs was quenched due to inner filter effect. Therefore, a ratiometric fluorescence strategy mediated by LS-CDs is used to realize quantitative detection of XOD. The carbon dots-catalyzed fluorescence sensing system was further used in cellular experiments with good results. This strategy has been shown to exhibit great potential in serum H2O2 and XOD detection and bioimaging.

First, 100 mg of sodium lignosulfonate and HCl solution (2 mmol, 1 mL) were added to deionized water (39 mL), and pretreatment brown solution was obtained by ultrasonication for 10 min. Then, pretreated brown solution was transferred to an autoclave and heat up at 180 ℃ for 10 h. Yellowish LS-CDs solution was produced after reaction. For product purification, the obtained suspension was centrifuged at 6500 rpm for 15 min and filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane. The filtrate was then dialyzed through a 3500 Da dialysis bag for 24 h. The dialysate was collected and concentrated. Finally, solid LS-CDs were collected by freeze-drying dialysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrogen peroxide and xanthine oxidase detection
ce:section.ce:para: The H2O2 assay was performed using following procedures: 50 μL of different concentrations of H2O2, 50 μL of OPD-CDs solution and 50 μL of LS-CDs, and 200 μL of acetate buffer (20 mM, pH 4.0) were mixed and incubated for 15 min at room temperature. 650 μL of Tris-HCl solution (50 mM, pH 8.0) was added thereafter. The fluorescence intensity was then recorded under 370 nm excitation.

The XOD assay was performed using following procedures: 50 μL of different active XODs and 50 μL of xanthine (10 mM) were mixed with 50 μL of phosphate buffer (pH 7.5, 10 mM) and incubated for 30 min at 37 ℃. Then, 50 μL of OPD-CDs solution, 50 μL of LS-CDs, and 200 μL of acetate buffer (20 mM, pH 4.0) were then added and incubated for 15 min under room temperature. Finally, 550 μL of Tris-HCl solution (50 mM, pH 8.0) was added. The fluorescence intensity was collected under 370 nm excitation after 1 min

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence detection xanthine oxidase in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Detailed information on human serum sample processing and cell culture and sensing experiments is described in the Supplementary Information.

The reasons for selecting lignin as precursors and the conjecture of CDs formation process are detailed in Supplemental Information. In addition, the lignin type and synthesis conditions were also considered. Due to the poor water solubility of alkali lignin and cellulolytic enzyme lignin under acidic conditions, it is difficult to fully react during acidification modification[20,35]. Sodium lignosulfonate has good water solubility under acidic conditions and is more suitable for acidizing modification. The temperature, time and HCl content of precursor synthesis are also optimized to obtain CDs with better fluorescence properties. Detailed optimization of synthetic conditions and possible explanations are given in Supplemental Information (Fig. S14). The LS-CDs were obtained under optimum synthesis conditions and subsequently characterized.

The LS-CDs showed monodisperse and spherical-like distributions ( Fig. 1 A). The LS-CDs showed a size distribution ranging from 2.0–5.2 nm with an average diameter of 3.4 nm (Fig. 1B). According to HRTEM, a clear lattice stripe was observed for LS-CDs. The lattice fringe is 0.21 nm, which corresponds to a graphite (100) structure[32]. The peaks near 3415 cm−1 were attributed to carboxyl and hydroxyl group vibrations (Fig. 1 C). The band near 1634 cm−1 was ascribed to CC stretching vibrations. More importantly, new vibrational peaks appeared at 1713 cm−1 and 668 cm−1, which were ascribed to CO vibration and C-Cl vibration[22]. These new vibrational peaks were not exhibited in CDs and LS, suggesting that HCl was served to modify CDs surface.

The crystallinity of LS-CDs was observed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) to further demonstrate the presence of graphite structure. Fig. S12A shows the XRD spectrum of LS-CDs. There is a broad diffraction peak near 2θ= 21°, which corresponds to the plane-matched axial lattice parameter of the graphitic material (002) [32], indicating that the LS-CDs have an amorphous graphitic structural structure. Therefore, the LS-CDs model is constructed with graphite as the core in the subsequent theoretical calculation.

The chemical structure of LS-CDs was further investigated by XPS. Fig. 1D shows the presence of peaks at 198.1, 285.1, and 532.0 eV, corresponding to Cl2p, C1s, and O1s, respectively, indicating that LS-CDs contain C, O, and Cl elements. The contents of these three elements are 2.6%, 70.2% and 27.2%, respectively, indicating that LS-CDs is mainly composed of carbon and oxygen and contains a small amount of chlorine. C1s high-resolution XPS could be decomposed into C-C/CC (284.7 eV), C-O (285.9 eV), C-Cl (286.8 eV), and CO (288.6 eV) (Fig. 1E). The XPS spectrum of O1s could be resolved into CO (531.3 eV) and C-O (532.6 eV) (Fig. 1 F). The XPS spectrum of Cl2p can be resolved as Cl2p3/2(198.0 eV) and Cl2p1/2 (199.7 eV), respectively (Fig. 1 G). In addition, a small amount of nitrogen was found in LS-CDs. Since lignin-derived CDs prepared without the addition of hydrochloric acid (pCDs) have low catalytic activity ( Fig. 2 C), it can be inferred that nitrogen is not the main active reaction site. Nitrogen may be contained in the production and preparation of sodium lignosulfonate, which will be one of the contents of the future work of this research group. In summary, HCl-mediated lignin CDs have been successfully prepared.

The optical properties of LS-CDs were studied. The appearance of absorption peak near 278 nm attributed to π-π * of sp2 conjugation domains (Fig. 1H). The absorption peak near 320 nm attributed to n-π * of CO, mainly causing fluorescence emission[23]. LS-CDs solution was pale yellow in daylight and showed bright blue color under 365 nm UV lamp. Fluorescence behavior of LS-CDs at different excitation wavelengths was further investigated (Fig. 1I) The fluorescence spectra of LS-CDs showed optimal excitation wavelength of 360 nm and maximal emission peak was at 455 nm. The fluorescence stability of LS-CDs was investigated. The fluorescence intensity of LS-CDs only changed by about 5% within 90 min of UV lamp irradiation, indicating strong resistance to photobleaching (Fig. S1A). Even in a high concentration of NaCl solution (100 mM), LS-CDs still maintained very good fluorescence performance, indicating strong salt resistance (Fig. S1B). The detailed calculation method is listed in the Supplemental Information. Taking quinine sulfate as a reference, the relative quantum yield of LS-CDs is calculated as 10.7%. LS-CDs retains high fluorescence in a mild aqueous solution (pH=6–10), while the fluorescence intensity is low under acidic conditions (Fig. S13). This may be due to the ionization of oxygen-containing groups on the surface of LS-CDs, resulting in changes in electronic states.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studies on the catalytic activity of LS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic activity of LS-CDs was investigated. Fig. 2 A is a schematic representation of TMB oxidation by LS-CDs and peroxidase. When LS-CDs and H2O2 were present in system, chromogenic substrates showed blue, yellow, and green colors, respectively (Fig. 2B). Due to high molar absorbance coefficient, TMB was employed for typical chromogenic substrate in catalytic reaction system (Fig. 2 C). In the absence of H2O2, LS-CDs cannot oxidize TMB into blue products. Therefore, LS-CDs do not have catalytic activity as oxidase enzyme. The unmodified CDs without hydrochloric acid have almost no peroxidase-like activity. It can be concluded that HCl-mediated modification greatly enhanced CDs-like peroxidase activity.

The UV absorption intensity at 652 nm varied significantly in HAc-NaAc buffer solutions at different pH values (3–6.5) (Fig. 2D). When the pH of the solution system is greater than 5.5, H2O2 will automatically decompose, resulting in poor oxidizing substrate capacity (Hu et. al., 2019). In addition, the diamine structure on TMB has poor solubility in weakly alkaline media. Therefore, acetic acid/sodium acetate buffer solution is used during the experiment to reduce the random error caused by pH. The optimal pH for catalytic reaction was 4.0. Zeta potentials of LS-CDs and CDs were measured − 7.3 eV for LS-CDs and − 1.6 eV for CDs. Under acidic conditions (pH=4.0), LS-CDs were more likely to bind to TMB via electrostatic interaction. Fig. 2E show that LS-CDs maintain strong peroxidase-like activity at 5–45 °C. which are less susceptible to temperature than horseradish peroxidase (0–40 °C) and have broader utility[33]. In addition, natural enzymes require proper temperature (below room temperature) and dry storage conditions. The stability of nanozymes is less affected by environmental, temperature and other factors, which is convenient for long-term retention and archiving, and has more advantages in traceability.

The mimetic enzyme activity of LS-CDs was also examined. The K m and V max of H2O2 were 0.32 mM and 19.53 × 10−7 M s−1, respectively (Fig. 2 F, Fig. 2 G). the K m and V max of TMB were 0.26 mM and 15.62 × 10−7 M s−1, respectively (Fig. 2H, Fig. 2I). As shown in Table S1 the V max values of LS-CDs were larger than those of the other reported peroxide mimetic enzymes. These results demonstrated that LS-CDs had strong peroxidase-mimicking activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studies on the catalytic site of LS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The existence of active site and catalytic mechanism of the LS-CDs were investigated by DFT. The fragmentation structure was optimized based on elemental content and bonding form characterization data of LS-CDs combined with the DFT-D3 dispersion correction method ( Fig. 3 A). Theoretical analysis of the active sites and catalytic mechanism of LS-CDs was carried out using B3LYP/6–31 G method in the Gaussian 09 program[24]. The simulated infrared spectra (Fig. S2) of the proposed LS-CDs structures showed significant bands at 3551, 1710, 1352, and 1120 cm−1, which were basically consistent with FT-IR experimental analysis of LS-CDs. Fig. 3B demonstrates the three-dimensional structure of LS-CDs containing atomic number. Fig. 3 C demonstrates Mulliken charge distribution of individual atoms of LS-CDs. Table S2 demonstrates Mulliken charge partition details of LS-CDs. It can be seen that the oxygen-containing functional groups carry more negative charges. The frontline molecular orbitals of LS-CDs were further investigated (Fig. 3D). The HOMO and LUMO orbitals of LS-CDs were mainly present on carboxyl, carbonyl, and chlorine groups interacting with carbon nucleus. The relatively low difference between HOMO and LUMO implies that the system is susceptible to electron leaps, which is more favorable for catalytic H2O2 decomposition by LS-CDs. Multiple LS-CDs structural theory calculation parameters are listed in Table S3, which facilitates a better understanding of surface fragmentation structure and catalytic performance. Fig. 3E demonstrates molecular electrostatic potential of surface fragments of LS-CDs. It can be seen that negatively charged red regions mainly appear at -COCl, -SO₃H, -CO, -COOH, and C-Cl of LS-CDs. These red regions can easily provide active electrons required for catalytic reactions, and thus these regions are likely to be potential active sites of LS-CDs. It is of interest that chlorine radicals originating from catalytic reaction generate an internal electric field that enhances charge carrier separation[25]. In this process, some of electrons further affect oxygen atom in carbonyl group, which ultimately promotes enhanced catalytic activity of LS-CDs[26].

Different free radical quenching agents were introduced to study the types of free radicals produced during catalytic process. Isopropanol (IPA), p-benzoquinone (BQ) and tryptophan (Trp) were adopted to remove·OH, O2 - and 1O2, respectively[27]. The catalytic capacity of IPA-containing system was obviously reduced, but the others were similar to those of blank group. (Fig. 3 F). It was initially concluded that·OH was produced during LS-CDs-induced catalysis, and electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra of LS-CDs+DMPO+H2O2 system displayed four characteristic peaks with intensity ratio of 1: 2: 2: 1, confirming·OH was present (Fig. 3 G).

The selective inactivation of oxygen-containing functional groups on LS-CDs was performed by chemical titration (Fig. 3H). Benzoic anhydride, phenylhydrazine, and 2-bromo-1-phenylethanol were adopted to specifically inactivate -OH, -COOH, and -CO functional groups, respectively[28]. Results indicated that LS-CDs (BA) were more active than LS-CDs and the activities of LS-CDs (PH), LS-CDs (BrPE) were lower than that of LS-CDs. It was reported that -OH in graphene inhibits catalytic activity whereas -COOH and -CO enhance catalytic activity[29]. The above results are consistent with conjecture that carbonyl group is a potential catalytic active site.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility study and xanthine oxidase assay sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of ratiometric fluorescence sensing strategy for XOD was investigated. XOD specifically oxidized substrate xanthine to produce H2O2, which was catalyzed by LS-CDs to generate·OH. The·OH subsequently enhanced fluorescence of OPD-CDs with LS-CDs fluorescence quenching ( Fig. 4 A). In this experiment, OPD-CDs+H2O2 coexisted to quench fluorescence of LS-CDs. It has been reported that active CDs with stable functional properties can be prepared by active structure retention strategy, and OPD-CDs retain fluorescence enhancement of OPDs after oxidation by horseradish peroxidase with H2O2 [30,31]. The possible mechanism of fluorescence changes was also discussed in Supplemental Information and main mechanism was inner filter effect (Fig. S3).

The construction of LS-CDs/OPD-CDs systems for H2O2 sensing behavior was further investigated to better expand cascade catalytic system applications. Fluorescence ratio F565/F454 of LS-CDs/OPD-CDs system increased significantly with increase of H2O2 concentration (Fig. 4B). What's more, F565/F454 showed a good linear relationship with H2O2 concentration from 1 to 150 μM (Fig. 4 C). The linear equation was expressed as y = 0.019x + 0.340 (R2 =0.995). The sensitivity of this assay was determined based on IUPAC recommended method[34]. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.27 μM according to the 3σ/k rule (σ is the standard deviation of the blank, and k denotes the slope of the standard curve). Results indicate that LS-CDs/OPD-CDs system is highly sensitive to H2O2. This offers possibility of realizing XOD trace detection. The LS-CDs/OPD-CDs/xanthin/xanthin oxidase system was studied under optimal conditions for XOD activity sensing (Fig. S4). The fluorescence ratio F565/F454 of LS-CDs/OPD-CDs/xanthin/xanthin oxidase system was gradually enhanced with the increase of XOD activity (Fig. 4D). F565/F454 showed a good linear relationship with XOD activity from 0.5 to 120 U/L (Fig. 4E). The linear regression equation was described as y = 0.016x+ 0.276 (R2 =0.996). The LOD value was calculated to be 0.13 U/L. Fig. 4 F reveals fluorescence photographs of LS-CDs/OPD-CDs/xanthin/xanthin oxidase system at different concentrations of XOD. Fig. 4 G reveals CIE chromaticity of LS-CDs/OPD-CDs/xanthin/xanthin oxidase system versus XOD concentration.

Possible mechanism of fluorescence changes was investigated. In this experiment, the relationship between the fluorescence spectra of LS-CDs and the UV absorption spectra of OPD-CDs was first examined. The overlap between the UV absorption spectra of OPD-CDs and the fluorescence spectra of LS-CDs was insignificant when H2O2 was not present. However, when H2O2 was present, LS-CDs catalyzed the production of·OH from H2O2 to oxidize OPD-CDs causing an increase in their absorbance near 420 nm. The UV absorption spectra of oxidized OPD-CDs overlapped significantly with the fluorescence spectra of LS-CDs (Fig. S3A). This suggests a possible inner filter effect or fluorescence resonance energy transfer between LS-CDs and oxidized OPD-CDs. The LS-CDs fluorescence lifetime changes were subsequently examined. When OPD-CDs and different concentrations of H2O2 were added, the fluorescence lifetime of LS-CDs was 3.63 ns (0 μM), 3.61 (50 μM), 3.58 (100 μM), 3.52 (150 μM), respectively (Fig. S3B). This indicates that the fluorescence lifetime of LS-CDs is almost constant before and after quenching. It has been reported that the fluorescence lifetime was unchanged during the inner filter effect while the fluorescence resonance energy transfer led to the decrease of fluorescence lifetime. Therefore, it is likely that the internal filter effect plays a dominant role in LS-CDs quenching.

In selectivity evaluation experiments, a number of co-existing substances that may be derived from biological samples have been tested. Other metal ions, anions, amino acids, and biomolecules hardly caused significant fluorescence changes. Fe3+ ions exhibited interference, most likely due to electron transfer with LS-CDs and its paramagnetic nature[30]. However, compared with XOD, the fluorescence quenching induced by these interfering substances was very weak because of its low concentration in serums (Fig. 4H). When in presence of XOD, Cys and GSH had a large effect on the system probably due to reducing nature of sulfhydryl group, which could be eliminated by adding N-ethylmaleimide (0.2 mM) to eliminate possible Cys and GSH interference in serum. Other interferences had negligible effects on LS-CDs/OPD-CDs/xanthin system (Fig. 4I). In enzyme cascade reaction, the catalytic product of each enzyme becomes the substrate of next enzyme, and so on, finally reaching the formation of the final product. In this experiment, xanthine was catalyzed by xanthine oxidase to produce hydrogen peroxide. Then, hydrogen peroxide is catalyzed by LS-CDs nanozyme to form hydroxyl radicals. Finally, the hydroxyl radical oxidizes the OPD-CDs to form oxOPD-CDs. Because of the specificity of enzyme and high specificity of enzyme cascade reaction, the enzyme cascade reaction can also produce signal amplification effect. Therefore, the system has high specificity for XOD and H2O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real sample detection and method comparison
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the applicability of ratiometric probe-based method, human serum samples was chosen as real samples to measure H2O2 and XOD. They were not detected in blank groups of serum samples from healthy people, indicating a very small concentration of them. These results were consistent with reports of fairly low H2O2 and XOD levels in serums from healthy people. In order to prove the accuracy of construction method, further spiking experiments on serum samples were carried out. Standard addition method was used to analyze three different serum samples, and results are presented in Table S4 and Table S5. The mean recovery of H2O2 was between 95.7% and 106.1%, and RSD was below 4.9%. The mean recovery of XOD was between 96.0% and 107.9%, and RSD was below 4.5%. The results of this method were compared with those obtained by conventional colorimetry (uric acid colorimetry), and results were consistent. However, traditional colorimetric method relies on the product uric acid, which produces precipitation at larger concentrations, resulting in insufficient sensitivity. Despite the small number of serum samples used, the method we developed has good advantages in terms of both anti-interference and accuracy, and can be used as a new xanthine oxidase assay for rapid and sensitive accurate early diagnosis of xanthine oxidase-related diseases.

A comparison was made between the proposed method and other H2O2 or XOD detection methods (Table S6 and Table S7). The LOD of proposed method is lower than that of most other methods. Though the LOD of proposed method is higher than a few fluorescence methods, it has a broader linear range. In terms of sensing methods, fluorescence methods enable real-time detection and non-invasive imaging of biological samples, leading to a more diverse range of biological sample applications. In terms of raw material selection, the method adopts renewable resource lignin without metal doping, thereby having good environmental protection, rich resources and low cost. Because of the characteristics of precursors, the method has good water-solubility and biocompatibility, and is more suitable for biosensor analysis. In terms of material preparation, bifunctional carbon dots were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method, which simplified steps of synthesis and modification. In addition, the ratiometric fluorescence strategy greatly reduces the interference of external factors on probe signal, making results more robust and reliable. The polychromatic nature of solution makes it possible not only to use spectrometers, but also to visually analyze it on a smartphone assistant platform. Scheme 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging studies of intracellular xanthine oxidase
ce:section.ce:para: Cytotoxicity assays were performed and the results are shown in Supplemental Information. Fig. 5 shows images of HeLa cells after 2 h incubation with LS-CDs/OPD-CDs. The cells exhibited bright blue fluorescence and weak green fluorescence (Fig. 5 A), which indicated that two CDs prepared were good candidates for cellular imaging. In the present sensing strategy, the content of H2O2 was crucial for the sensing results, and thus the intracellular H2O2 sensing imaging capability was further investigated. Incubation into cells of a reagent that induces hydrogen peroxide production from inflammation in HeLa cells, fopperol 12-tetradecanoate 13-acetate (PMA), was used to increase the endogenous H2O2 content of cells. In another set of experiments, the H2O2 scavenger N-acetylcysteine (NAC) was incubated into cells. After PMA treatment, the fluorescence signal of the blue channel decreased, while the green fluorescence increased. (Fig. 5Ab), which was compatible with those of extracellular addition of H2O2. After treatment with NAC, blue fluorescence was restored and green fluorescence was significantly attenuated (Fig. 5Ac). It was attributed to the fact that NAC scavenges H2O2 in vivo, thereby inhibiting·OH production. Compared with untreated cells, the fluorescence image after XOD treatment showed stronger green fluorescence and weaker blue l fluorescence (Fig. 5Ad). A possible explanation is that XOD reacts with xanthine to generate H2O2 which subsequently generates·OH to oxidize OPD-CDs. after NAC treatment, blue fluorescence was restored and green fluorescence was significantly weakened (Fig. 5Ae). The fluorescence intensity of blue and green channels in cell images was obtained by software tool. In the selected area, the fluorescence intensity distribution maps of the five groups of cells were shown in Fig. 5B and Fig. 5 C, respectively. The fluorescence intensity ratios of green and blue (RatioG/B) were 0.17, 2.12, 0.94, 1.71, and 0.34 in Fig. 5D. It is concluded that the ratiometric fluorescence strategy is applicable to H2O2, XOD imaging and sensing.

In this experiment, LS-CDs were prepared for the first time without additional metal doping by using sodium lignosulfonate as a carbon source and hydrochloric acid as a modifier.LS-CDs not only have good optical properties, but also have excellent peroxidase-like catalytic activity. The materials have significant advantages in terms of price, storage conditions, and action conditions rather than natural enzymes. The catalytic mechanism and active sites were discussed by combining DFT theory and experiment. The results indicated that carbonyl and carboxyl groups on surface of modified LS-CDs may be the main active sites, and the catalytic reaction was based on·OH. Inspired by the bifunctionality of LS-CDs, the ratiometric cascade catalytic sensing system was constructed by introducing OPD-CDs. The constructed system showed excellent sensing performance for H2O2 and XOD. The strategy has been successfully used for sensing XOD in serum and living cells. A chemical-enzymatic cascade biocatalytic system was constructed to realize system signaling. This work provides potential applications for biochemistry, disease diagnosis, and tumor therapy.

Wu Meng: Methodology. Sun Qijun: Investigation, Data curation. Chen Ligang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Niu Na: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Yu Xueling: Investigation. Yin Chenhui: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was funded by Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (LH2022B002), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2572022DJ01), 111 Project (B20088), Heilongjiang Touyan Innovation Team Program (Tree Genetics and Breeding Innovation Team). We would like to acknowledge the technical support from Analysis and Testing Center of Northeast Forestry University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.135458.

ce:section.ce:para:

.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Chenhui Yin is a Doctor at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. His major is biology and his research direction is biological analysis.

Qijun Sun is a Doctor candidate at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. His major is biology and his research direction is chemical biology.

Meng Wu is a Doctor candidate at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. Her major is biology and her research direction is chemical biology.

Xueling Yu is a Master student at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. Her research direction is fluorescent probes and their visualization applications.

Na Niu is an associate professor at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. Her main research direction is fluorescent probes and their practical applications.

Ligang Chen is a professor at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. His main research directions are fluorescence probes and molecular imprinting techniques.
